own_review.issue: False-positive leak reporting: ResultSets that are auto-closed by Statement.close() per the JDBC spec are now counted as leaks. StatementWrapper.close() -> closeTrackedResultSets() counts every still-open ResultSetWrapper and adds it to connection.leakedResultSets, so idiomatic code (close the Statement, rely on cascade close of its ResultSet) now fires a 'JDBC resources leaked' warning at connection close. The test change confirms the regression: resultSetLeak() now adds statement.close() and STILL expects the leak warning. Old code only counted ResultSets of statements that were themselves leaked.

Exception swallowing changes semantics two ways: (1) if next.isClosed() throws (StatementWrapper/ResultSetWrapper delegate to the live driver object, which can throw), the count++ and next.close() are both skipped -- the underlying resource is silently never closed and not reported; (2) previously closeTrackedStatements() propagated SQLException from statement.close() out of ConnectionWrapper.close(); now all close failures are silently swallowed.

Plain non-volatile int with non-atomic '+=' increment. Statements of the same connection can be closed from different threads (the replaced ConcurrentLinkedQueue implies that expectation), so updates can be lost and visibility is not guaranteed. Affects diagnostics only.

Memory retention: closed wrappers stay linked until the parent clears the list. A long-held connection executing many statements accumulates StatementWrapper objects O(statements-created) instead of O(statements-open); same for ResultSet wrappers per statement and enlisted ConnectionWrappers per transaction. Acknowledged in the PR description but unbounded in the worst case.

Warning message grammar: 'Closing open connection(s) on after completion'. Also warnings are now fired once per batch instead of once per wrapper, losing the count information previously implied by repeated warnings.

Edge race: an element inserting itself concurrently with closeAllAutocloseableElements() can CAS onto the freshly-reset head after traversal started; it is then only closed on the NEXT clear. For a Statement's trackedResultSets, there is no next clear after the statement's final close, so such a ResultSet wrapper is never closed by tracking. Narrow window, unusual usage pattern.
Human reference point: class javadoc could better document the per-state logic, invariants and ordering guarantees of the lock-free list (insertion vs reset linearization).

points.note: Claims the new implementation lacks closed-state filtering and 'MUST track a closed state... exclude closed elements' -- closeAllAutocloseableElements() already does exactly that via next.isClosed(). Properly-closed wrappers are NOT counted. The real false-positive defect (ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close counted as leaks) has a different mechanism v4 never identifies. Absence-fabrication.

The method is deleted by the PR; the claim that it 'is called from closeTrackedStatements at line 125' is false for the final code. Speculation from the truncated diff presented as a required change. Absence-fabrication.
Hedged 'verify' request, no concrete defect; the CAS insert/reset logic is in fact correct.
Verified: PR adds a 99-line lock-free concurrent class with zero dedicated unit tests; only an existing integration test is tweaked.

points.loc: StatementWrapper.java:94-108 memory retention of closed wrappers
points.note: Verified real (matches known defect): closed wrappers remain referenced until parent close; unbounded for long-lived connections.

points.loc: ConnectionHandler.java:107 enlisted wrapper not removed on close
points.note: Accurate observation but self-resolved as 'likely acceptable'; closed wrappers are skipped via isClosed at completion.
Generic verification request, not a finding.
Verification request; the diff already shows both removals.

missed: False-positive leak reporting for ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close() (the actual mechanism: StatementWrapper.close counts still-open RS wrappers into leakedResultSets)
Unsynchronized non-atomic leakedResultSets counter in ConnectionWrapper
Exception swallowing in closeAllAutocloseableElements skipping close() when isClosed() throws, and loss of SQLException propagation from connection.close()
Human point: document logic/invariants/ordering in AutoCloseableElement

points.note: Verified real (matches known defect): plain int '+=' loses updates under concurrent statement close; severity correctly assessed as low (diagnostics only).

Verified accurate and insightful: the test now exercises 'RS leaked, statement closed' instead of 'statement+RS leaked'; correctly notes the original path lost coverage. Stops short of calling out the JDBC-spec false-positive, but the observation is real.

points.loc: ConnectionWrapper.java closeTrackedStatements possible missing null guard / NPE
points.note: The 'if (trackedStatements != null)' guard and the JDBC_RESOURCES_NOT_LEAKED return are plainly present in the full diff v5 saw. Hedged insinuation of a defect that does not exist. Absence-fabrication.
Confirms the code is correct; not a finding.

points.loc: ConnectionHandler.java transactionBeforeCompletion count not in warning
points.note: Minor diagnostics suggestion; also slightly inaccurate -- old code fired one warning per wrapper, so a count was implicit.

missed: False-positive leak reporting for ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close() (v5 describes the test change but treats the new leak accounting as intended rather than a regression)
Memory retention of closed wrappers in the linked lists until parent close
Exception swallowing in closeAllAutocloseableElements skipping close() when isClosed() throws, and loss of SQLException propagation
Human point: document logic/invariants/ordering in AutoCloseableElement
exceeds: Test coverage semantics shift in resultSetLeak()

qualitative: The human reference is thin (one documentation nit), so both machine reviews exceed it on substance, but both also fabricate. v4, working from a truncated diff, invented its headline 'critical' finding twice over: it demands closed-state filtering that closeAllAutocloseableElements already performs and prescribes changes to trackedResultSetSize(), a method the PR deletes -- two absence-fabrications -- while legitimately catching the memory-retention issue and the missing unit tests. v5 is more grounded: it correctly identifies the non-atomic leakedResultSets counter and gives a genuinely sharp analysis of the resultSetLeak test-semantics shift, but it hedge-fabricates a missing null guard that is visible in the full diff it was given. Both candidates miss the most user-visible regression -- ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close() now reported as leaks -- and the exception-swallowing in closeAllAutocloseableElements that can skip close() entirely. Net, v5 edges out v4 on accuracy per claim despite finding fewer things.
